sentences.text: Yes.
That was wonderful, that we did have those breaks to normality till the professors themselves were then deported.
And then, of course, nothing till new lot were arrived and had to be sort of-- some people couldn't do it anymore or didn't want to.
But anyway, I didn't have very much free time with those three children and trying to get-- get them-- oh, yes.
One of the jobs that I did for a long time after I left-- left the kitchens is the laundry.
I thought, that's a place I want to be.

If I can keep the kids clean, and have baths, and get at the soap, that I was-- it was all-- although, it was all so horrible and frightening, it was also rather like a great adventure, stimulating adventure that I had to get on top of it and not get under it.
So it wasn't sort of so-- it was always a rather adventurous bit to it.
Yeah, a challenge.
That's right.
And so I got in this laundry.
And I was in my element there, because there was lots of hot water.
And every night, I was-- I could have a shower before I went back, a hot shower with all the soap.
I had to steal the soap, mind you.
It wasn't a nice bit of soap.
It was as I passed where the laundry, there was all these-- that soap was all in-- what you call it?
Powder, powdered stuff.
So as I passed, I didn't want anybody to see.
And I grabbed a handful.
I held onto it until I got into the shower.
So I had soap.
It wasn't nice soap, it was-- but it was soap.
At least it was better than no soap.
And I had that.
Every night, I had a shower.
And-- also, I could get my washing done for my children.
So I always had clean things to wear.
There weren't irons or anything.
But, you know, I could give somebody some food.
And they would put my washing in with a lot of other washing, and then get it out for me before it was noticed.
You know, it was always with food.
So I always had to be near food or able to get food.
If I did somebody else's washing, they would give me food.
I would be able to pass that food onto somebody who would do something for me.
Well, they were supposed to be being taught.
They were supposed to be in a school.
And that was set up so that some people who were schoolteachers at least wouldn't be sent to be gassed.
They would have jobs in that Theresienstadt.
Or, I mean to say, there were all these men without women.
You get held with some little boy and goodness knows what would happen.
So you were always nervous that they didn't want them wandering around alone.
So that was all right.
And then, because I had three children, I was allowed to leave at 1 o'clock.
I think we started at 5:00 in the morning.
I was up unearthly hour.
But that never worried me getting up early.
So 1 o'clock, I would-- I went to fetch my children.
So-- and then, I produced-- I produced what food I was able to manage to scrape together, or somebody giving me, or whatever.
And I didn't give them-- I had nothing.
I only had a bowl, or a mug, or something.
And they'd stand in front of me.
And I used to say, you know, here's a spoonful for you, and a spoonful for you, and a spoonful till the mug was empty.
Well, because I was thin, always been thin, I had a small tummy and I didn't need to fill it.
So I-- I never had all the terrible pains that other people had.
Real stomach aches they used to have, because there's nothing in their tummies.
I had hunger pains, but not so violent that I-- you know, I just had to put up with it.
And I did, but never as bad as other people.
I was always very grateful that I had such a tiny tummy.
It didn't have to be filled all the time.
Drank a lot of water, of course, which was good, if the water's clean.
Yes.
They all had-- I think they must have caught it.
They had-- what's this thing that children always have?
Chickenpox and the other pox, the bad one, Smallpox.
I had-- I caught smallpox.
And I was very ill with smallpox.
I thought I'd never survive.
But I did.
Well, yes.
There were doctors that came.
I can't even remember if there was anything.
I don't think they could do much, really.
You just had to rest.
So all you-- rest didn't cost anything.
We were stuck up on the three tier thing on the top waiting to get better.
Yes.
Yes.
Wood.
Hard wood with a blanket, not much comfort.
Well, it was supposed to be the place where you didn't get sent on to be gassed.
It was a-- so you know, you were grateful that you were there.
And thank god we were-- I think-- I think if that war had lasted much longer, they-- we would have been sent on, too.
And instead of sort of protecting us, they came in and raped us.
Because they were all soldiers and there were all these women there unprotected.
So, of course, they let rip.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I hid.
I wasn't going to be raped by a Russian.
And I wasn't.
Thank goodness.
And so I said, well, I want to get out of here.
I don't like it.
It's too dangerous.
A lorry came and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] oh, wait a minute.
No, there was-- yeah, there was an English captain got himself a little wooden hut, an office, made himself an office in the camp where he sort of got things organized.
And I thought, that's the man I want to get hold of.
At last, I could speak English to, you know, my own people.
And he said, well, you know.
Oh, he said, I've got an idea.
But I can't tell it to you now.
Come back tomorrow.
And I had great difficulty getting to this place, because it was guarded.
And you couldn't get into his thing.
Somehow or other, I managed to slip in and get a hold of him.
And he said, come back tomorrow.
So I thought, oh, I only hope I'm able to come back.
It's not easy.
No, it wasn't a captain.
He was just an ordinary soldier.
And he started talking to me.
And as soon as he heard me speak, he knew that it wasn't a cooked up story.
He had a plane.
That's right.
I had to get to the air field.
I really can't tell you how I managed to get out of that camp and to that air field.
But anyway, I got to these English flyers and their plane.
I got in the plane with my three kids.
Oh, yes.
And then they said to me, we're not flying to England.
We've flying to-- we've got to fly to France.
I think they were going to see some French girls.
So we would have to spend the night in France somewhere, which wasn't-- it was another, you know, thing that-- not nearer home.
Anyway, they went off to their girls.
And I went-- I don't know where it was.
They plunked us and said, we're coming for you tomorrow morning early.
And that's what they did.
They came at about an unearthly hour, collected us in a Jeep or something, drove to the air field, I remember, in France.
We got in a plane.
And believe it or not, we did fly over the channel and landed in-- I can't remember the airport anymore, the name.
But we landed in England.
But-- they landed me somewhere in a field near the airport.
And said, look, we can't take you in, because we're going to get into trouble for this.
So here you are.
You wanted to be in England.
You're in England.
Now, the rest is up to you to get out.
Oh, I think-- that's right.
They went off.
And I did see them again after, believe it or not.
But that's another story.
I think I'd tell them the truth.
I said, I've arrived here.
Somebody got me over.
I'm not allowed to talk about it, but I want to get to my father.
My father is the music publisher, Keith [INAUDIBLE], in London.
You can ring him up.
And I told him the telephone of regent 6,000.
I can still remember it, good heaven.
And ring him up and tell him to come and fetch me here, because I-- I'm here with his three grandsons.
And that's what they did.
They got me.
Well, I can't remember how it was.
But I got-- my-- my father came to fetch me.
That's how it was.
He came to fetch me.
A coach.
That's all he could get.
So he came alone in the coach.
And there we were in this big coach with-- and the children went, hello, [INAUDIBLE].
Because they couldn't speak English.
I didn't want them to speak English.
And my father was quite overwhelmed by it all.
And he took us back to his flat.
He only had a one-room flat.
So he had to try and find a place for us to live.
And he was marvelous, I must say.
He did find us somewhere, I can't remember.
And I think another flat was empty there.
And so we all crowded into one room somewhere.
But we thought it was fantastic to be free.
They were very kind.
And they got some clothes.
It was quite difficult to get clothes in England.
It was all on the ration thing.
And of course, my next aim was to get-- I didn't want to stay in England.
I wanted to get to Holland, to my house, see if I could get back into my house again as soon as the Germans had left Holland.
And that was what the next step was eventually.
The children did go to school in England.
And they learned English very quickly.
And I had this house, a horrible house it was, of a basement.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
I never got in the house again.
The house had been sold and sold and sold to different people.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I don't know.
My mind's gone blank now.
I can't seem to remember what happened.
Yes.
I got back to England very soon.
But how-- why?
I think because there was nothing left in Holland.
I tried to get my money.
And I think in the end, I succeeded.
The Dutch government behaved very well towards people that had managed to survive it.
And they-- I got some money back, a considerable sum, of course.
And then I think-- well, I didn't seem to want to stay in Holland, too many memories, nasty memories.
So I came back to England.
My father was marvelous.
And the children were then sent to boarding school, so at least I was-- you know, got a bit of freedom.
And then I tried to get a job.
I did some sort of social work.
Well, of course, as the war faded, they set up offices that you could apply to have traced what happened to them.
And it was then-- what you got out of it was presumed dead in Auschwitz.
Yes.
Before she was taken, she didn't-- see, I wasn't going to be parted from my children.
Whatever happened or wherever we went, I thought, if we go to hell, I'm going with the children to hell.
I'm not leaving my children.
Her idea was quite different.
She said, if she had to go to the camp, she was not going to take her child.
So she managed-- she had wonderful friends in the country.
And they took this little girl and brought her up as their child.
She-- she was very young, so she learned to speak Dutch.
And she was given a different name, their name.
And then, somehow or other-- oh, that's right.
I think these people then, after the war, applied-- they had different bureaus set up to find people.
And they applied to this bureau to try and find this child's parents.
And, of course, the only parents that appeared was me, her sister.
And then they got into touch with me and said, we've got Ruth-- Ruth Woolff.
And I said, oh, well, send her to me, because it'd be lovely for the boys to have a sister.
If I had three children or four children, it didn't make much difference to me.
Had a big family, so that was lovely.
She came, she fitted in very well.
And the boys were lovely to her.
They weren't jealous or nasty or-- they behaved very well.
And she's always been with them ever since.
Yes.
I have spoken to her about her parents, because I thought I should, too.
But she didn't seem to be very interested.
She was so young that that parent thing was sort of-- wasn't anything that she could understand.
The only parent she had was me that she could understand.
And it was the same for the boys.
You know, they hadn't got a father.
So it was quite normal for her to be with somebody who hadn't got a father, but had got a mother.
Yes.
They were together.
She and her husband hid-- were hidden somewhere together and found, discovered together, and taken away together.
Yes.
Lovely.
Well, I was pleased to be in England.
I was home.
I never particularly liked Holland.
They didn't like me.
Well, they thought I was stuck up.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I wasn't Dutch.
Although, they liked the English.
Well, I was at a disadvantage.
I had no home and no money.
And, you know, you're not very popular then in a war and Jewish as well.
So there was not much to recommend me.
Yes, I was.
And I never wanted to leave it again, didn't want to go on long holidays or-- there we are.
Well, they've all-- touch wood-- turned out fine.
They're not great high flyers.
They've not got marvelous positions or earning a lot of money, but they're decent, you know, decent people, law-biding citizens, I'd say.
They've got jobs.
I mean, they're not directors of things, but-- oh, well, Nick went to-- old Nick, the eldest, he stayed in Holland.
He was very Dutch-orientated.
He loved Holland.
And so he went into the Dutch Navy till he got his pension and was still there to the end.
He never married.
No.
What-- where is he?
Yes, he's in Australia.
That's right, when he retired.
He retired to-- he liked Australia.
It's big, and he can roam.
He's-- he never-- he's one of these people who can't settle down.
He's always on the move.
And Steven and Carel married very nice English girls, not Jewish, of course.
And well, got families, and they've got homes.
And they've got jobs.
And they're very content and happy.
And they're very sweet to me.
And all's well with the world.
Oh, that was very sad.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE].
My second husband-- I'm sorry that I get mixed up with them.
The second husband, David.
That's right.
Well, I wasn't-- I had a long affair.
I will confess that.
Because he was married.
And he didn't want to divorce his wife.
And I didn't want him to divorce her.
I didn't want that on my conscience.
I thought having a secret affair was better than nothing.
And as long as we were careful and didn't hurt the wife, I could put up with that.
Anyway, that's what went on for a long, long time.
About 20 years, believe it or not.
And then the wife conveniently died, I must say.
And so he was freed.
And so we did get married in the end, but we were only really properly married for about three years, and then they had a heart attack one morning.
And within 10 minutes, he was dead.
He died in my arms.
It was terribly sad.
I couldn't believe it.
I knew he had-- well, he was old.
He was-- I think he was 80 or something, so it wasn't-- you know-- anything that wasn't imminent or [INAUDIBLE] for many years already.
And that was the end of my life, that sort of life.
Oh, about 11 years now, quite a while ago.
But it seems quite recent.
I'm always thinking about him.
Well, I've sort of lived for the children, I suppose, and the grandchildren and tried to make something of it.
Tried to get them homes and their own homes, so they didn't pay rent but lived in-- managed to get-- put down money for the-- for the lease thing.
You know, so they've got their own places and can't be chucked out.
They're only small, little flats, two-room flats or three-room flat.
But they're all happy and contented and got nice children.
And these children are already grown up and have left home.
They've got partners as they're now called.
One is married and got a son.
So I've got one great grandson of which I'm very proud.
And so I've got a huge family.
